title: aion-worker (Rust) task receive loop and bounded concurrency
purpose: Build the heart of the Rust worker: the loop that pulls tasks off the server-streamed receive call, decodes the task envelope, dispatches each to a bounded pool of concurrent executions, and feeds results back to the session for reporting. This is the runtime that turns a connected session into a serving worker.
task: Implement the worker loop in runtime/loop_.rs and the task envelope decode in protocol/task.rs. The loop consumes the WorkerSession receive stream, decodes each ActivityTask (activity type, ActivityId, attempt, input Payload), and spawns its execution onto a bounded pool sized by WorkerConfig.max_concurrency, applying backpressure on the stream when the pool is saturated. Do not implement handler invocation/classification (AR-003), heartbeat (AR-004), or reconnect (AR-005) here — the loop calls into a dispatch seam those briefs fill.

requirements.title: ActivityTask envelope decode
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL decode a received task into an ActivityTask exposing the activity-type name, ActivityId, attempt number, and the input Payload with its content-type tag.
acceptance: protocol/task.rs defines an ActivityTask struct (or thin wrapper over the aion-proto task message) exposing activity_type, activity_id (aion-core ActivityId), attempt: u32, and input: Payload.
Decoding preserves the Payload content-type tag exactly (no coercion), per CO7.
A malformed task message maps to WorkerError::Decode with the cause preserved.
A unit test decodes a representative aion-proto task message into an ActivityTask and asserts every field, including the content-type tag.

requirements.title: Worker loop: receive and dispatch seam
requirements.spec: WHILE the session is connected, THE SYSTEM SHALL consume the receive-task stream, decode each task, and hand it to a dispatch seam for execution, collecting results to report through the session.
acceptance: runtime/loop_.rs defines the loop that drives the WorkerSession receive stream to completion (or until shutdown).
Each received task is decoded (R1) and passed to a dispatch entry point whose implementation lands in AR-003 (the loop depends on a trait/fn seam, not a concrete handler invoker).
Completed and failed outcomes from dispatch are routed back through WorkerSession::report_result / report_failure.
A test using the in-memory fake session feeds two tasks and asserts both are dispatched and their outcomes reported in correspondence to their ActivityIds.
The loop logs (via tracing) each task received and reported without logging payload contents.

requirements.title: Bounded concurrency and backpressure
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL execute at most WorkerConfig.max_concurrency activities simultaneously and SHALL apply backpressure on the receive stream when the pool is saturated. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT spawn executions unbounded.
acceptance: The loop limits in-flight executions to max_concurrency using a semaphore or equivalent bounded primitive.
When max_concurrency executions are in flight, the loop stops pulling new tasks from the stream until a slot frees (backpressure), rather than buffering unboundedly.
A test sets max_concurrency = 2, feeds 5 slow tasks, and asserts no more than 2 execute concurrently (e.g. via a shared atomic gauge with an asserted peak of 2).
On shutdown, no new task is pulled and the bounded pool drains before the loop returns (drain semantics finalised in AR-006; the loop exposes the drain hook).

boundaries: SHALL NOT invoke typed handlers or classify failures — that is AR-003 (the loop calls a dispatch seam).
SHALL NOT send heartbeats (AR-004) or implement reconnect (AR-005).
SHALL NOT define the Worker builder or shutdown signal source — AR-006 (the loop exposes a drain hook AR-006 wires).
verification: cargo test -p aion-worker passes, including the concurrency-cap test.
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.
